questions.text: In the context of expert witness testimony, what is the precise definition of 'foundation' as it relates to the admissibility of an expert's opinion?
options: A. The expert's personal financial interest in the outcome of the case
B. The preliminary evidence establishing that the expert has sufficient knowledge, skill, experience, training, or education to render an opinion on the matter
C. The written contract between the expert witness and the retaining attorney
D. The expert's prior testimony history in similar cases

questions.text: What is the technical distinction between a 'fact witness' and an 'expert witness' in the context of fraud litigation?
options: A. Fact witnesses testify about events they personally observed, while expert witnesses may testify about matters within their professional domain based on specialized knowledge
B. Fact witnesses are always more credible than expert witnesses in fraud cases
C. Expert witnesses can only testify about financial matters, while fact witnesses testify about all other subjects
D. Fact witnesses are compensated by the court, while expert witnesses are paid by the parties

questions.text: Under Federal Rule of Evidence 702, which of the following BEST defines the standard for admitting scientific, technical, or specialized knowledge?
options: A. The testimony must be based on reliable principles that have been published in peer-reviewed journals within the last five years

B. The testimony is admissible if it will assist the trier of fact to understand the evidence or determine a fact in issue, and is based on sufficient facts or data, reliable methods, and reliable application of those methods
C. The testimony must be corroborated by at least two independent sources before it can be admitted
D. The testimony is admissible only if the expert has testified previously in at least three other cases

questions.text: What is the precise meaning of 'bias' in the context of assessing an expert witness's credibility in a fraud case?
options: A. A systematic inclination or prejudice toward a particular conclusion, often stemming from financial interest, professional affiliation, or prior relationship with a party
B. The expert's educational background and academic credentials
C. The number of times the expert has been retained by the same attorney
D. The expert's failure to use the most current technology in their analysis

questions.text: In litigation context, what is the specific definition of 'hearsay' as it may affect an expert witness's reliance on documents?
options: A. A statement made by a person other than the testifying witness, offered to prove the truth of the matter asserted
B. Any statement made by an attorney during the litigation process
C. Information that an expert witness obtained from informal conversations with colleagues
D. All documents that were not created specifically for purposes of litigation

questions.text: Which term BEST describes the requirement that an expert witness's methodology must be subject to testing and potential refutation?
options: A. Falsifiability principle, derived from Daubert and applicable to assessing the reliability of expert opinions
B. Chain of custody requirement
C. Materiality threshold
D. Relevance doctrine

questions.text: In expert witness practice, what is the technical definition of a 'hypothetical question'?
options: A. A question posed to an expert asking them to assume certain facts as true and provide an opinion based on those assumed facts
B. Any question that the expert cannot answer based on their current knowledge
C. A question designed to trick the expert into contradicting their report
D. A question about matters that occurred before the expert was retained

questions.text: What is the precise meaning of 'expert exception to the hearsay rule' in Federal Rule of Evidence 703?
options: A. Experts are completely exempt from all hearsay restrictions and may testify about anything they have heard
B. An expert may base an opinion on inadmissible hearsay if it is of a type reasonably relied upon by experts in the field, even if the hearsay itself is not admitted into evidence
C. Experts may only rely on documents that have been authenticated by the opposing party
D. Hearsay evidence becomes admissible only when an expert witness is present during trial

questions.text: In the context of fraud investigations, what is the specific definition of 'materiality' as it applies to expert testimony?
options: A. The physical substance or composition of evidence examined by the expert
B. The requirement that an expert's opinion address facts or issues that would affect the determination of the case or influence a reasonable person's decision
C. The expert's ability to access all documents related to the litigation
D. The timeline of events that the expert has investigated

questions.text: What is the technical distinction between 'ultimate issue testimony' and 'opinion on a legal conclusion' in expert witness practice?
options: A. Ultimate issue testimony addresses the final outcome of the case, while legal conclusion testimony addresses what the law requires; experts may generally testify on the ultimate issue but not state legal conclusions
B. Ultimate issue testimony is always permitted, while legal conclusion testimony is never permitted
C. These terms are synonymous and refer to the same type of testimony
D. Legal conclusion testimony is permitted only in criminal cases, while ultimate issue testimony applies only in civil cases

questions.text: In expert witness terminology, what is the precise meaning of 'qualified privilege' in communications with the retaining attorney?
options: A. A legal protection that may shield communications between an attorney and expert from discovery under attorney work product doctrine, subject to certain conditions
B. An absolute guarantee that all expert communications will remain confidential
C. The expert's right to refuse to answer any question posed during deposition
D. A requirement that all expert communications must be in writing to maintain confidentiality

questions.text: What is the specific definition of 'impeachment' when applied to an expert witness in litigation?
options: A. A formal charge brought against the expert by the court for misconduct
B. The process of attacking or discrediting an expert's credibility, reliability, or opinion through cross-examination or presentation of contradictory evidence
C. A requirement that the expert withdraw their opinion before the conclusion of trial
D. The expert's admission that they made a calculation error in their analysis

questions.text: In the context of CFE expert witness practice, what is the precise definition of 'professional standards' as they apply to fraud investigations and testimony?
options: A. Guidelines established by professional organizations such as the ACFE that define the expected level of competence, ethics, and methodology in fraud investigation and expert testimony
B. Requirements imposed solely by the court system for all expert witnesses
C. Recommendations that experts may follow at their discretion without any enforcement mechanism
D. Standards that apply only to expert witnesses who have been certified by government agencies

questions.text: When distinguishing between embezzlement and theft in expert witness testimony, which characteristic is most critical to establish liability in civil proceedings?
options: A. The perpetrator's intent to permanently deprive the owner of property
B. The fiduciary relationship between the perpetrator and the victim organization
C. The total monetary value of assets misappropriated over the offense period
D. The method of concealment used in the fraudulent scheme

questions.text: In comparing Ponzi schemes to pyramid schemes during expert testimony, what is the most significant operational difference that should be emphasized?
options: A. Ponzi schemes involve multiple layers of recruitment, while pyramid schemes rely on a single investor
B. Ponzi schemes pay returns from new investor money rather than legitimate business operations, whereas pyramid schemes may generate some legitimate revenue
C. Pyramid schemes are exclusively illegal, while Ponzi schemes may operate within regulatory frameworks
D. Ponzi schemes target institutional investors, while pyramid schemes target retail investors

questions.text: When testifying about financial statement fraud detection, how should an expert witness differentiate between aggressive accounting and intentional misstatement for liability purposes?
options: A. By examining the consistency of accounting methods across multiple reporting periods
B. By analyzing management's intent, the materiality of adjustments, and the presence of corroborating documentation
C. By comparing the company's accounting practices to industry standards exclusively
D. By reviewing the auditor's management letter comments from prior years
questions.tag: Financial Statement Fraud vs. Aggressive Accounting

questions.text: In expert witness testimony regarding digital forensics, how does the chain of custody requirement for electronic evidence differ from physical evidence documentation?
options: A. Digital evidence does not require chain of custody documentation if the source is clearly identified
B. Digital evidence requires additional documentation of access logs, hash values, and imaging procedures to establish authenticity and integrity
C. Physical evidence requires more rigorous chain of custody than digital evidence in litigation
D. Chain of custody requirements are identical for both digital and physical evidence under all circumstances
questions.tag: Digital Evidence & Chain of Custody Distinction

questions.text: When comparing occupational fraud perpetrated by employees versus external fraud perpetrators, which factor most significantly impacts the expert witness's assessment of internal control effectiveness?
options: A. The employee's tenure with the organization and job performance evaluations
B. The bypassing of internal controls by those with authorized access versus exploitation of control gaps
C. The total loss amount and recovery potential in each scenario
D. The geographic location where the fraudulent activities occurred

questions.text: In fraud litigation, how should an expert witness distinguish between circumstantial evidence and direct evidence when establishing motive in a financial crime case?
options: A. Circumstantial evidence proves motive directly, while direct evidence requires inference
B. Direct evidence (such as confessions or documents showing intent) establishes motive more definitively than circumstantial evidence (such as financial pressure or lifestyle changes)
C. Circumstantial evidence is inadmissible in civil fraud cases, while direct evidence is always admissible
D. Both types of evidence are equally probative and require no distinction in expert testimony

questions.text: When testifying about corruption schemes, how does a bribery arrangement differ from a kickback arrangement in terms of the relationship between parties and transaction structure?
options: A. Bribes involve public officials exclusively, while kickbacks involve private transactions only
B. Bribes are payments for future favorable action, while kickbacks are typically payments for business referred or transactions completed
C. Bribes are always larger in value than kickbacks in commercial contexts
D. Kickbacks are legal in most jurisdictions, whereas bribes are universally prohibited

questions.text: In expert witness testimony regarding revenue recognition fraud, what distinguishes a fictitious sales scheme from channel stuffing in terms of audit trail and documentary evidence?
options: A. Fictitious sales leave no audit trail, while channel stuffing creates complete shipping and invoice documentation
B. Channel stuffing involves legitimate sales with excessive quantities, while fictitious sales typically lack supporting documentation such as shipping records or customer confirmations
C. Both schemes produce identical documentary evidence requiring identical expert analysis
D. Fictitious sales always involve customer collusion, while channel stuffing does not

questions.text: When comparing misappropriation of assets through check tampering versus ACH fraud in expert witness analysis, which procedural safeguard is most effective at preventing each respectively?
options: A. Dual authorization controls prevent both check tampering and ACH fraud equally
B. Check tampering is best prevented by signature verification and check stock controls, while ACH fraud requires transaction authentication and recipient bank verification protocols
C. ACH fraud cannot be prevented through procedural safeguards, only through insurance coverage
D. Both schemes are prevented exclusively through employee background screening

questions.text: In litigation testimony, how should an expert witness explain the distinction between fraud and negligence regarding materiality thresholds and culpable knowledge requirements?
options: A. Fraud and negligence have identical materiality thresholds and knowledge requirements under tort law
B. Fraud requires knowledge or reckless disregard of truth with intent to deceive, while negligence involves failure to exercise reasonable care without fraudulent intent
C. Negligence claims require higher materiality thresholds than fraud claims in all circumstances
D. Materiality is irrelevant in fraud cases but critical in negligence determinations

questions.text: When testifying about vendor fraud schemes, how do shell company schemes differ from overbilling schemes in terms of documentation manipulation and invoice authenticity?
options: A. Shell company schemes create entirely fraudulent vendors with fabricated documentation, while overbilling schemes use legitimate vendors but manipulate invoice amounts or quantities
B. Both schemes involve identical documentation and require identical detection methodologies
C. Overbilling always involves more documentation than shell company schemes
D. Shell companies must register with tax authorities, while overbilling schemes avoid all government scrutiny

questions.text: In expert witness analysis of occupational fraud cases, how should the expert differentiate between financial statement fraud and asset misappropriation regarding financial impact and accounting treatment?
options: A. Financial statement fraud only affects income statement accounts, while asset misappropriation only affects balance sheet accounts
B. Asset misappropriation is recorded through fraudulent journal entries, while financial statement fraud involves only unrecorded transactions

C. Financial statement fraud typically inflates reported earnings or assets through accounting manipulation, while asset misappropriation directly reduces company assets through unauthorized transfers
D. Both fraud types have identical impacts on financial statements and require identical expert analysis
questions.tag: Financial Statement Fraud vs. Asset Misappropriation

questions.text: When testifying about the detection of occupational fraud using analytical procedures, how should an expert witness explain the difference between anomaly detection and trend analysis in identifying potential fraudulent transactions?
options: A. Anomaly detection identifies unusual or unexpected transactions deviating from established baselines, while trend analysis examines patterns and changes over time to establish normal behavior expectations
B. Trend analysis is more effective than anomaly detection for all fraud detection purposes
C. Both methodologies are identical and produce equivalent results in fraud investigations
D. Anomaly detection can only be applied to digital transactions, not physical assets

questions.text: A forensic accountant is retained as an expert witness in an embezzlement case. During the initial engagement, the attorney requests that the expert focus the investigation only on transactions that support the client's theory. What is the most appropriate response by the expert witness?
options: A. Accept the scope limitation as the attorney is paying for the investigation
B. Decline to accept the engagement or insist on conducting an independent, unbiased investigation regardless of the client's theory
C. Agree to focus on supporting evidence but privately investigate other areas
D. Proceed with the investigation but only document findings that are favorable to the client

questions.text: In a civil litigation matter, an expert witness is asked during cross-examination: 'Isn't it true that you are being paid a substantial fee to testify in this case?' How should the expert respond to avoid appearing biased?
options: A. Refuse to answer the question as it is irrelevant to the expert opinion
B. Acknowledge the fee transparently and clarify that the fee is for professional services, not contingent on the outcome
C. Minimize the amount and change the subject to the expert's credentials
D. State that the fee is confidential information protected by attorney-client privilege

questions.text: An expert witness has identified a significant calculation error in their initial expert report that actually undermines the expert's own conclusion. The expert is two weeks away from trial testimony. What action should the expert take?
options: A. Wait until cross-examination to explain the error as part of testimony
B. Immediately notify the retaining attorney and prepare an amended report or supplemental disclosure
C. Make a minor adjustment to the calculation and maintain the original conclusion
D. Discuss the error only with the attorney and not disclose it to opposing counsel

questions.text: During expert witness testimony in a fraud case, the opposing counsel asks the expert: 'What percentage of your annual income is derived from expert witness work?' The expert witness earned $250,000 last year, with $200,000 from expert witness engagements. Which response best protects credibility?
options: A. 'I don't believe that information is relevant to my opinions in this case'
B. 'Approximately 80 percent, though I also maintain an active accounting practice'
C. 'That information is confidential between myself and my accountant'
D. 'A significant portion, but my opinions are based solely on the evidence'

questions.text: A CFE is preparing an expert report on a Ponzi scheme. The retaining attorney requests that the expert highlight only the most damaging evidence against the defendant and minimize alternative explanations. How should the expert proceed?
options: A. Comply with the attorney's request to strengthen the client's case
B. Include all material findings, acknowledge limitations, and present alternative explanations objectively
C. Submit two reports: one for the attorney and a censored version for disclosure
D. Refuse to work on Ponzi scheme cases due to their complexity

questions.text: During a deposition, an expert witness is questioned about reliance on hearsay statements from company employees regarding internal controls. Under Federal Rule of Evidence 703, when can an expert rely on such hearsay?
options: A. Never, as hearsay is inadmissible in all circumstances
B. Only if the employee is also available to testify at trial
C. If the type of hearsay is of a kind reasonably relied upon by experts in the field, even if inadmissible in evidence
D. Only with written permission from the opposing counsel

questions.text: A forensic accountant discovers during trial preparation that a key document used in forming their expert opinion has been substantially altered since the expert first reviewed it. How should the expert witness respond?
options: A. Disregard the alteration and proceed with testimony based on the original analysis
B. Immediately inform the retaining attorney and discuss the impact on the expert's opinions
C. Report the alteration to the opposing counsel directly without informing the retaining attorney
D. Exclude this document from the expert report to avoid complications

questions.text: When an expert witness is cross-examined on the assumptions underlying their fraud analysis, opposing counsel challenges whether those assumptions are reasonable. What is the best approach for the expert to maintain credibility?
options: A. Defend all assumptions as unchallengeable based on the expert's experience
B. Acknowledge the reasonableness of the challenge and explain how alternative assumptions would affect conclusions
C. Refuse to discuss assumptions as they are protected by work product doctrine
D. Admit that the assumptions may be incorrect and therefore the entire opinion is unreliable

questions.text: An expert witness in a securities fraud case is asked during direct examination to explain the 'red flags' indicative of financial statement manipulation. Which approach best demonstrates expertise while maintaining objectivity?
options: A. Cite only the red flags present in this specific case to prove fraud occurred
B. Discuss red flags generally from professional literature, explain which are present in this case, and acknowledge that red flags alone do not prove fraud
C. List all possible red flags without distinguishing their significance or applicability
D. Avoid mentioning red flags that are absent from this case

questions.text: During expert witness engagement, the CFE is informed that the retaining attorney intends to pay the expert's fee from settlement proceeds if the case is won. What should the expert do?
options: A. Accept this arrangement as it aligns the expert's interests with the client
B. Decline the engagement or insist on a fixed fee structure independent of litigation outcome
C. Accept conditionally, but require double the fee to account for the risk
D. Request that the arrangement be kept confidential from opposing counsel

questions.text: An expert witness prepared a detailed analysis of cash flow anomalies suggesting fraud. At deposition, opposing counsel reveals that the expert misunderstood the company's accounting system and the 'anomalies' are actually standard procedures. How should the expert respond?
options: A. Insist the analysis is correct regardless of the accounting system used
B. Acknowledge the misunderstanding, explain how it affects the conclusions, and offer to revise the analysis
C. Claim the information about the accounting system is not relevant to the expert's opinion
D. Request a recess to consult with the retaining attorney before responding

questions.text: When testifying about the methodology used to detect fraud indicators, an expert witness is asked: 'Isn't it possible that non-fraudulent companies exhibit similar patterns?' What is the most credible response?
options: A. 'No, these patterns are unique to fraud situations only'
B. 'Yes, it is possible. These indicators raise questions but must be evaluated in context with other evidence'
C. 'That's a legal question, not an accounting question for me to answer'
D. 'I cannot speculate about hypothetical situations'

questions.text: In preparation for expert witness testimony, an expert reviews prior testimony transcripts from previous cases. When opposing counsel asks if the expert's opinions in the current case differ from prior cases, what is the appropriate response?
options: A. Deny reviewing prior testimony to avoid appearing inconsistent
B. Acknowledge the review and explain any differences based on different facts, assumptions, or evolution of professional knowledge
C. State that each case is independent and prior testimony is irrelevant
D. Refuse to discuss prior testimony as it violates confidentiality

questions.text: An expert witness in a complex financial fraud case discovers that new forensic accounting evidence contradicts a key assumption in their previously submitted report. The case is scheduled for trial in two weeks. What is the most appropriate course of action?
options: A. Immediately notify the retaining attorney and prepare a supplemental report addressing the discrepancy before trial
B. Wait until cross-examination to address the new evidence, as it demonstrates thorough analysis
C. Assume the opposing counsel will discover the contradiction and prepare a rebuttal strategy
D. Maintain the original report position, as changing expert opinions appears inconsistent to the jury

questions.text: During expert witness testimony in a whistleblower retaliation case, opposing counsel presents financial records that the expert had not reviewed during case preparation. The attorney asks the expert to explain discrepancies in their analysis. What should the expert witness do?
options: A. Attempt to distinguish the new documents as irrelevant without examining them carefully
B. Acknowledge unfamiliarity with the documents, explain their relevance if appropriate, and offer to review and opine if qualified to do so
C. Admit that the new documents invalidate the entire expert opinion already provided
D. Decline to comment, citing the attorney's failure to produce the documents during discovery

questions.text: A CFE expert witness is retained in a case involving allegations of embezzlement by a mid-level manager. During testimony, the expert provides a range of estimated losses rather than a specific figure. The opposing counsel argues this demonstrates uncertainty and lacks precision. How should the expert respond?
options: A. Provide a single specific number to satisfy the court's apparent demand for certainty
B. Explain that loss estimation ranges reflect reasonable professional judgment given data limitations, and cite industry standards supporting this methodology
C. Admit that the analysis is too preliminary to support any definitive conclusions
D. Argue that the opposing counsel's challenge is inappropriate and refuse to clarify further

questions.text: In preparing for testimony in a healthcare fraud case, an expert witness learns that the retaining attorney has withheld certain exculpatory evidence from opposing counsel in violation of discovery rules. The attorney asks the expert to avoid mentioning this evidence during testimony. What is the expert's ethical obligation?
options: A. Follow the attorney's instructions, as the expert's role is to advocate for the retaining party
B. Immediately withdraw from the case and report the attorney to the bar association
C. Refuse to participate in concealment and advise the attorney of the disclosure obligation; if unheeded, consider withdrawal and potential reporting
D. Mention the evidence only if directly questioned about it during cross-examination

questions.text: An expert witness in a complex insurance fraud investigation provides testimony regarding statistical analysis of claims patterns. The opposing expert challenges the dataset selection, arguing that excluding certain outliers biases the analysis. How should the testifying expert address this criticism?
options: A. Defend the original methodology without acknowledging any potential limitations
B. Explain the rationale for data selection criteria, discuss how outlier treatment affects results, and acknowledge if the excluded data would materially change conclusions
C. Admit that statistical analysis is inherently subjective and therefore unreliable
D. Propose that the jury should ignore statistical evidence in favor of anecdotal testimony

questions.text: During a deposition in a Ponzi scheme case, opposing counsel asks the expert witness about conversations with the retaining attorney regarding strategy and approach to the analysis. The expert is uncertain whether these communications are protected by attorney-client privilege. What should the expert do?
options: A. Answer all questions to demonstrate that the expert has nothing to hide
B. Refuse to answer and instruct opposing counsel to consult with the retaining attorney regarding privilege claims
C. Provide selective information about conversations while withholding strategy discussions
D. Answer only after the retaining attorney has explicitly waived any privilege claims

questions.text: An expert witness testifies in a case involving alleged manipulation of revenue recognition practices. The expert's analysis relies heavily on internal e-mail communications as primary evidence. During cross-examination, opposing counsel questions whether the expert has appropriate expertise to interpret complex accounting standards referenced in the e-mails. How should the expert respond?
options: A. Admit that interpretation of accounting standards is beyond the expert's scope and disregard the e-mail evidence
B. Assert that any literate person can understand e-mails regardless of accounting expertise
C. Explain the expert's background in accounting standards, clarify which interpretations fall within professional competence, and acknowledge limitations where appropriate
D. Shift focus to the content of e-mails rather than addressing the underlying accounting principles

questions.text: A CFE retained as expert witness in an embezzlement case discovers during case preparation that the client company's internal controls were substantially weaker than initially represented. This discovery undermines the expert's initial assessment that the fraud would have been easily detectable. What is the appropriate response?
options: A. Revise the analysis to reflect the actual control environment and report these findings to the retaining attorney
B. Proceed with the original assessment to maintain consistency with the client's narrative
C. Resign from the case without explanation to avoid appearing to support weak conclusions
D. Limit discussion of internal controls in testimony to avoid embarrassing the client

questions.text: During expert witness testimony in a mortgage fraud case, the expert references academic literature and industry studies to support opinions on lending practices. The opposing counsel argues that reliance on peer-reviewed sources is inappropriate because the expert should rely only on personal experience. How should the expert respond?
options: A. Agree that personal experience is the only valid basis for expert opinions
B. Explain that authoritative literature, industry standards, and peer-reviewed research are recognized methods for developing reliable expert opinions and enhance credibility
C. Distinguish between the expert's personal observations and literature-based conclusions without explaining why both are relevant
D. Acknowledge that literature may be helpful but assert that the jury should weight it less heavily than personal experience

questions.text: An expert witness in a financial statement fraud case is asked during trial whether the expert has changed any opinions since the written report was submitted six months prior. The expert has not materially changed opinions but has refined certain calculations based on additional discovery. How should this be communicated?
options: A. State that no changes have been made to avoid appearing inconsistent
B. Detail only the most significant refinements while minimizing minor adjustments
C. Transparently explain which aspects of the analysis have been refined and the basis for refinements, distinguishing between opinion changes and methodological improvements
D. Defer all questions about modifications to the retaining attorney

questions.text: In a complex fraud case involving international transactions, an expert witness relies partly on translation and interpretation of foreign documents provided by a third-party translator not retained directly by either party. Opposing counsel challenges whether the expert is qualified to vouch for translation accuracy. What is the most appropriate response?
options: A. Guarantee the translation accuracy based on the presumed expertise of the translator
B. Acknowledge reliance on the third-party translator, describe the basis for confidence in that source, and clarify that opinions are based on the translated content as received
C. Refuse to rely on any translated materials and demand that all documents be re-translated at trial
D. Claim that translation accuracy is not relevant to the expert's ultimate opinions

questions.text: An expert witness is retained to analyze whether a company's auditor should have detected a material fraud based on the audit work performed. The auditor's firm contests the expert's engagement, arguing that hiring an expert to critique audit procedures is itself problematic. How should this challenge be addressed?
options: A. Withdraw from the case to avoid the appearance of impropriety in critiquing other professionals

B. Acknowledge the sensitivity of audit criticism but explain that independent expert evaluation of audit adequacy is standard in fraud litigation and does not constitute improper attack on the profession
C. Agree that audit procedures should not be questioned and redirect analysis to operational controls instead
D. Hire a separate expert to analyze the first expert's conclusions about audit procedures

questions.text: During trial testimony in a healthcare billing fraud case, the expert witness presents a demonstrative exhibit showing loss calculations that was created by a graphics designer hired by counsel, not by the expert. The opposing counsel questions whether the exhibit accurately represents the expert's analysis. What should the expert testify?
options: A. State that the designer's work is identical to the expert's analysis and vouch for every design choice made
B. Explain that the exhibit was created by a third party and therefore the expert cannot guarantee its accuracy in all respects

C. Review the exhibit carefully before trial, verify its accuracy to the underlying analysis, and testify to its faithful representation while acknowledging that design elements were created by graphics professionals
D. Refuse to address the exhibit's accuracy since it was not personally created by the expert
